data.title: Gold drifts lower with eyes on US-Iran developments
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump says Iran war 'close to over'
  • Oil little changed, Hormuz shipping constraints persist
  • Fed's Goolsbee says rate cuts may need to wait until 2027

By Ashitha Shivaprasad

Gold drifted lower on Wednesday after hitting a one-month peak as investors assessed the latest signals on the U.S.–Iran situation and what they could mean for the interest rate outlook.

Spot gold was down 0.7% at $4,807.34 per ounce as of 11:22 a.m. ET (1522 GMT), after hitting its highest since March 18 earlier in the session. U.S. gold futures fell 0.4% to $4,830.60.

"Gold and silver are just seeing some mild and routine profit-taking after scaling overnight highs," said Jim Wyckoff, senior analyst at Kitco Metals.

"Gold prices have been rallying on improved risk appetite and selling off during bouts of risk aversion in recent sessions, running counter to the metal's traditional safe-haven role. Traders are currently more focused on the implications of tighter monetary policy and inflation pressures," he added.

U.S. President Donald Trump said the war with Iran was close to over, telling the world to brace for an "amazing two days", as the army chief of mediator Pakistan arrived in Tehran in a bid to prevent a renewed conflict.

Oil prices were little changed as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remained constrained. Forty-five days after Iran's Revolutionary Guards declared the strait closed, transit through the waterway remains uncertain despite a two-week ceasefire.

The Federal Reserve may need to wait until 2027 to cut interest rates if an extended bout of high oil prices from the Iran war delays inflation's progress towards the U.S. central bank's 2% goal, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said on Tuesday.

The market currently sees a 30% chance of a U.S. rate cut this year. (FEDWATCH)

Higher interest rates tend to weigh on gold by increasing the opportunity cost of holding a non-yielding asset, offsetting the metal's appeal as an inflation hedge.

Among other metals, spot silver held steady at $79.58 per ounce, while platinum rose 0.2% to $2,108.79. Palladium was down 1.2% at $1,568.15.

data.title: Peruvian assets lead LatAm markets lower on election uncertainties
data.description:
Key points:
  • Peruvian assets slump amid election uncertainty and fraud allegations
  • Venezuela sanctions eased by US, but officials call for full removal
  • Argentina's inflation rises to 3.4% in March, surpassing analyst forecasts

By Johann M Cherian

Peruvian assets dived on Wednesday, as uncertainties prevailed over election outcomes in the Latin American country, while broader caution prevailed over emerging markets as investors mulled the outlook of tensions in the Middle East.

The copper-rich country entered its fourth day of vote counting with no clear second candidate to face conservative leader Keiko Fujimori in a presidential runoff, official data showed, with about 90% of ballots counted.

However, as counting continued, allegations of fraud have started to spread more widely. Lopez Aliaga and Sanchez have shown concerns about the integrity of the process with Lopez Aliaga sharing a post that called for Keiko to request the proceedings to be annulled.

MSCI's benchmark for the country's share index (.MIPE00000PUS) fell 5% and was on track for its biggest one-day drop in over one month. A U.S.-listed exchange traded fund (.EPU) tracking the country's equities lost 3.5%, while mining companies Buenaventura and Southern Copper fell 4.8% and 1.8%, respectively.

The local sol currency depreciated 1.3% to a one-week low and hard-currency bonds (US715638DW93=TE), (US715638DU38=TE) were down about 0.5 cents on the dollar.

"They have a broken political system where the voters have no interest in who is running for what, so no wonder it's so messy and complicated," Eduardo Ordóñez Bueso, an emerging markets debt portfolio manager at BankInvest.

Bueso added that Keiko is seen as a more market friendly candidate, given her right leanings and since her father, Alberto Fujimori, used to run the country.

CAUTION OVER MIDEAST

MSCI's index tracking equities in the region (.MILA00000PUS) slipped 0.7%, having rallied 7% over the past five sessions, while a parallel currencies gauge (.MILA00000CUS) dipped 0.3%, although it was near a record high.

Global markets have been steadily recovering losses incurred since the Iran-U.S. conflict started in late February as traders took comfort from reports that a second round of talks between the countries was anticipated after the first set of negotiations failed.

However, the United States military imposed a blockade on Iranian ports and a report said that more U.S. troops were to be sent to the region, reminding investors that geopolitical tensions were still high.

Brazil's real slipped 0.1% and Colombia's peso declined 0.8%, while the currencies of Mexico and Chile were steady.

On the equities front, Brazil's Bovespa index lost 0.5%, while Colombian and Mexican equities dipped 1% and 0.2%, respectively.

Meanwhile, the Trump administration issued two new Venezuela-related general licenses, including one that allows financial transactions involving certain Venezuelan banks and Venezuelan government individuals.

However, the country's interim President Delcy Rodriguez called for U.S. sanctions to be removed, adding that the licenses did not provide long-term legal security.

Prices of the country's defaulted bonds (VE050485153=), (USP17625AE7=1M) were marginally higher

Argentina's MerVal index lost 1%. Data showed that the country's monthly inflation rate was 3.4% in March, up from the 2.9% registered in February and above analysts' forecast of 3.0%.

Elsewhere, brokerage HSBC upgraded Hungarian equities to "overweight" from "neutral" adding that it would be doing this by increasing their underweight exposure in ASEAN.

The country's assets have rallied this week as investors bet on a slew of reforms under the newly elected Tisza government on hopes that it could result in greater integration with the European Union and the unlocking of pandemic-era funds.

data.title: W.Africa Crude-Differentials stay elevated
data.description:
Crude differentials for West African grades continued to hold strong on Wednesday as traders awaited loading programmes from Angola for June.

  • Nigerian crude grades Qua Iboe and Bonny Light are being offered at around dated Brent plus $10 and in the low double-digits, a trader said. Details of any traded levels did not emerge.

  • Traders were also awaiting the release of Angolan loading programmes for June as soon as later this week. Around 9-10 cargoes from the current program were still available but didn't need to be sold, the source added.

  • In the wider market, state-owned oil firm Sonangol has not received any formal communication from Botswana signalling its desire to acquire a stake in Angola's new $6.6 billion Lobito refinery, a senior company executive said late on Tuesday, after widespread media reports quoting Botswana's energy minister that they were weighing options to secure a 30% shareholding in the plant once it comes online.

  • Two U.S. administration officials told Reuters exclusively that the country would not renew a 30-day waiver of sanctions on Iranian oil at sea that expires this week, and quietly let a similar waiver on sanctions on Russian oil expire over the weekend. China and India - key buyers of West African crude - have turned to buying Iranian crude at a cheaper rate than other grades.

  • Nigeria's oil production has risen to 1.8 million barrels a day, Finance Minister Wale Edun told Reuters.

data.title: Colombia central bank board could face difficulties functioning due to government absence
data.description:
Colombia's central bank board could face operational difficulties following the government's withdrawal from the body, board chief Leonardo Villar said on Wednesday, suggesting a review of the bank's regulatory framework.

Finance Minister German Avila announced his resignation from the Central Bank's board at the end of March, where he served as the government's representative and chairman, after disagreeing with the bank's decision to raise the benchmark interest rate by 100 basis points to 11.25%.

The minister's decision was supported by the country's president, Gustavo Petro, who has repeatedly criticized the central bank for raising rates.

Villar also asked the government to tone down its rhetoric against the bank and its board members.

data.title: Trump-Backed WLFI Proposes Massive Token Burn Amid Justin Sun's ‘Backdoor’ Feud
data.description:
Key points:
  • World Liberty Foundation proposed locking 63.28 billion tokens for at least two years through a new governance plan.
  • Founder and team-held tokens would face a two-year cliff, followed by a three-year vesting schedule and a 10% burn of around 4.52 billion WLFI tokens.
  • Around 17.04 billion early supporter tokens would be locked in for 2 years, followed by a 2-year vesting period, with no burn. 

Trump family-backed World Liberty Foundation (WLFI) announced on Wednesday a bold proposal to overhaul its token locks amid the swirling controversy with Tron (TRX) blockchain founder Justin Sun. 

The firm, which lists President Donald Trump as its co-founder emeritus, said on X it has posted a new governance proposal that will lock away 63.28 billion tokens for at least the next two years. https://x.com/worldlibertyfi/status/2044391016103322028

If approved, the 45.24 billion WLFI tokens that are with the founder and founding team would be subject to a two-year "cliff", then a three-year gradual vesting. Around 10% of the tokens, up to 4.52 billion, will be burnt permanently – or permanently removed from the token supply. Around 17.04 billion early supporter tokens would be locked in for 2 years, followed by a 2-year vesting period, with no burn. 

WLFI’s token fell nearly 2% in the last 24 hours to around $0.08. Retail sentiment around the cryptocurrency on Stocktwits trended in ‘bullish’ territory over the past day, accompanied by ‘extremely high’ levels of chatter.

TRON Founder Justin Sun Raises Governance Concerns

The decision to lock away tokens and strengthen the supply chain follows Sun's accusation that the company treats the crypto community as its “personal ATM.” In a post on X, Sun said WLFI wallets were being frozen due to a “hidden backdoor” built into the WLFI token, undermining the decentralized ethos of the crypto community. 

In late 2025, WLFI’s anonymous team froze Sun’s wallet, which held roughly over 500 million WLFI tokens, after a small transfer of about 9 million WLFI. Sun alleged that the transfer was done without communication or due process. 

Earlier this week, Sun called on World Liberty Financial to disclose control of its governance keys, alleging that one wallet can unilaterally freeze user assets. He said on-chain data shows centralized control undermines claims of decentralization, with real authority concentrated in an anonymous address and a small multisig group.

“Who is that person? The community deserves an answer,” he wrote in a post on X. “If the WLFI team has nothing to hide, they should have no difficulty identifying who controls these keys.”

The WLFI token has fallen over 75% from its record high of $0.33 seen in September. Over the past year, the blockchain platform launched its in-house stablecoin USD One (USD1) and applied for a national bank charter. Retail sentiment around USD1 on Stocktwits trended in ‘neutral’ territory over the past day, accompanied by ‘extremely high’ levels of chatter.

data.title: PNC Gives Update on Private Business Lender Exposure — WSJ
data.description:
By Ben Glickman

$33 billion

That's PNC Financial's exposure to private business lenders, including private-credit funds.

The bank said that $7 billion of that figure is in the form of loans to private-credit providers, a focus of jittery investors in recent months. The remainder, $26 billion, is in the form of trade receivables securitization and other asset-backed securitization (allowing businesses to turn future cash flows, like accounts receivables, into immediate cash).

Executives emphasized on a call with analysts that the bank's exposure to non-bank loans is low risk, and that they expect no losses going forward.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Euro zone yields steady as traders weigh prospect of fresh U.S.-Iran talks
data.description:
By Sophie Kiderlin

Euro zone bond yields were broadly steady on Wednesday as hopes grew that peace talks between the U.S. and Iran would resume and traders weighed up the prospect of a resolution emerging for the Middle East conflict.

U.S. President Donald Trump said the war with Iran was close to over, as the army chief of mediator Pakistan arrived in Tehran in a bid to prevent a renewed conflict.

But elsewhere, U.S. forces imposing a blockade turned back vessels leaving Iranian ports.

Germany's 10-year government bond yield (DE10YT=RR), the benchmark for the euro zone, was last up one basis point (bp) at 3.04% after having declined in the previous session. In late March it hit 3.13%, its highest level since 2011.

Bonds have been under pressure throughout much of the conflict that sent energy prices higher, sparking inflation concerns and prompting markets to sharply adjust their central bank interest rate expectations.

European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde on Tuesday said the central bank was not yet in a position to determine if the current oil-price-driven inflation shock is transitory or if it requires the bank to raise interest rates.

Euro zone inflation jumped to 2.5% in March from 1.9% a month earlier as higher oil and gas costs drove up prices, according to flash data.

Referring to Lagarde's remarks, Kenneth Broux, head of corporate research for FX and rates at Societe Generale, said "obviously she wouldn't commit to a move in April, but I think reading between the lines it tells me that she's not quite ready to raise rates yet."

Money markets were last pricing in at least two interest rate hikes from the ECB in 2026, with a small chance of a third. Chances of a rate hike at the central bank's next meeting in April have meanwhile eased and were last at around 25%, down from 50% on Monday.

"The importance of central bank rhetoric today is as important as it has ever been," Justin Onuekwusi, chief investment officer at St. James's Place, said, adding that markets will hang on to every word from policymakers.

The yield on the German 2-year government bond (DE2YT=RR), which is more sensitive to policy rate expectations, was last down one bp to 2.54%. It fell more than 9 bps in the previous session.

data.title: Winmark 10-Q: Revenue $20.85M, EPS $2.50 (diluted)
data.description:
Winmark reported quarterly revenue of $20.85 million and diluted earnings per share of $2.50, with net income of $9.25 million reflecting a modest decline from the prior year. Revenue was pressured by reduced leasing income while royalties increased, and the effective tax rate for the period was 22.0% influenced by tax benefits from option exercises. Operating results were supported by higher royalty income despite increased selling, general and administrative expenses.

Financial Highlights

  • Total revenue: $20.85 million (down from $21.92 million prior year)
  • Income from operations: $12.36 million (operating margin 59.3% of revenue)
  • Net income: $9.25 million (prior year $9.96 million)
  • Effective tax rate: 22.0% (tax benefit from option exercises reduced rate)
  • Earnings per share — basic: $2.59
  • Earnings per share — diluted: $2.50

Business Highlights

  • Revenue mix shift: Royalties became the primary revenue driver after leasing income declined and the leasing portfolio ran off; royalties rose 8.4% year-over-year.
  • Franchise footprint: Total franchised stores increased to 1,383 with net openings across key brands, indicating ongoing brand momentum.
  • Franchise agreements: Strong renewal activity with 23 of 23 available agreements renewed, signaling franchise stability.
  • Operating costs: SG&A increased 5.8%, primarily due to higher compensation, partially offsetting operating income gains.

Original SEC Filing: WINMARK CORP [ WINA ] - 10-Q - Apr. 15, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: This Big Bull and Bear Agree on One Thing: The Market Thinks the War is Over — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Connor Smith

It's hard to get stock market bears and bulls to agree on anything, but two prominent, eponymous economists finally found common ground this week.

Rosenberg Research's David Rosenberg, a prominent bearish commentator, and Yardeni Research's Ed Yardeni, a so-called perma-bull, both made the case in recent notes that the stock market believes the war in Iran is coming to an end.

Rosenberg titled his Wednesday morning note, "Risk-on Markets Have Priced in the War's End — What Next?" while Yardeni published "Mr. Market Says The War Is Over" late Tuesday evening.

Both arguments are based on the fact that the S&P 500 is now up 1.3% from its close the Friday before Iran's supreme leader Ali Khamenei was killed by U.S. and Israeli strikes.

"It has also been another momentum-led rebound, similar to last year's explosive rally that started on April 9, when President Donald Trump postponed his Liberation Day tariffs," Yardeni writes, referring to last year's spring relief rally.

Rosenberg also cites the rally in riskier assets as a sign the market thinks the war is pretty much over. He points out that energy stocks have "virtually retraced their wartime gains," and adds that "even the bond market is back on an even keel after yesterday's rally."

This week's market gains have come even though this past weekend's talks between the U.S. and Iran didn't yield a peace deal. Instead, the U.S. began a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, which could further disrupt global oil supplies. But hopes that both sides will return to the table and reach a deal have the S&P 500 back near its highest levels on record.

"Investors clearly are looking through the proverbial valley, and why not, since the president stated that he thinks we could have a deal by the end of the week," Rosenberg writes. "Though we have heard that before."

He notes that Israel and Lebanon are also holding diplomatic talks, though the odds of a breakthrough are low.

"In any event, things are far less bleak than they appeared to be last weekend," Rosenberg adds.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average was down 200 points, or 0.4%, Wednesday morning. The S&P 500 was up 0.4% and on track for a fresh record close. The Nasdaq Composite was up another 1% after closing higher 10 days in a row.

While the market might be turning from the war, there are other risks for Wall Street to consider.

"While the war may be cooling down, inflation is heating up, led by rising energy costs," Yardeni writes. "The question is, will these costs put upward pressure on core inflation?"

And he's supposed to be the bull! Of course, Yardeni does cite the March core producer price index, which came in cooler than feared this week.

"In our Roaring 2020s base case scenario, strong productivity gains will keep unit labor costs subdued in 2026," Yardeni writes. "Also helping to offset the energy shock in the coming months should be the fading of last year's tariff shock and the ongoing moderating of rent inflation."

That's more like it.

Write to Connor Smith at connor.smith@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
